title: 1.1 Equations and solution sets
description: Read a linear system as a set of conditions and describe its full solution set clearly.

body.raw:
## What we are really looking for

A linear system is not just a pile of equations. It is a list of conditions that a
single choice of numbers must satisfy all at once.

If a pair or tuple of numbers makes every equation true, that choice is a
solution. The collection of all such choices is the solution set.

<Definition title="Solution set">
A solution set is the collection of every number or vector that satisfies the
whole system.
</Definition>

## One system, one meaning

For a system such as

$$
x + y = 4, \qquad x - y = 0,
$$

we are looking for numbers that make both equations true at the same time.
The second equation says the two numbers must be equal. Then the first equation
forces that common value to be `2`.

So the solution set is `{(2, 2)}`.

<WorkedExample title="A tiny system with one solution">
Solve

$$
x + 2y = 5, \qquad x - y = 1.
$$

From the second equation, `x = y + 1`. Substitute that into the first equation:

$$
(y + 1) + 2y = 5.
$$

That gives `3y = 4`, so `y = 4/3`. Then `x = 7/3`.

The solution set is `{(7/3, 4/3)}`.
</WorkedExample>

## Why solution sets matter

Later we will rewrite systems in matrix form. Even then, the goal does not
change. We are still trying to describe the same solution set, just with a more
efficient language.

Before moving on, try translating one system into its augmented matrix. Matching
each equation to one row makes the later row operations feel much less
mysterious.

<InteractiveWidget id="system-augmented-matrix-explorer" />

## Common mistake

<CommonMistake title="One equation is not enough">
It is not enough to make only one equation true. A solution must satisfy every
equation in the system.
</CommonMistake>

## Quick check

<QuickCheck prompt="Which ordered pair solves both equations?">
Try `(1, 3)`, `(2, 2)`, and `(4, 0)` for the system `x + y = 4`, `x - y = 0`.
</QuickCheck>

<RevealSolution title="Answer">
Only `(2, 2)` works, because it makes both equations true at the same time.
</RevealSolution>

## Read this first

If you want a slower reminder about what a matrix entry means later on, move to
[2.1 Matrix basics](/en/notes/math1030/matrices/matrix-basics).
